blocks.body: Adjectives and Adverbs are an important part of English grammar. Here you will learn how to use adjectives to describe people and things, adverbs to describe actions, form degrees of comparison (comparative and superlative), and understand adjective order and other descriptive patterns.

• Adjectives describe nouns: a beautiful flower, an interesting book.
  • Position: before a noun or after to be: The car is fast.
  • Form: they do not change for number or gender: one big house, two big houses.
  • Order: Opinion → Size → Age → Color: a beautiful big old blue car (simplified order).
blocks.heading: Adjectives

blocks.body:
• Adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs: She speaks quickly.
  • Formation: we often add -ly to an adjective: slow → slowly, careful → carefully.
  • Position: usually after the verb or at the end of the sentence: He drives carefully.
  • Adverbs of frequency (always, often, never) usually go before the main verb: I always study.
blocks.heading: Adverbs

blocks.body:
• Comparative is used to compare two things: bigger, more beautiful.
  • Superlative highlights one item in a group: the biggest, the most beautiful.
  • Short adjectives: add -er/-est: fast → faster → fastest.
  • Long adjectives: use more/most: interesting → more interesting → most interesting.
  • Irregular forms: good → better → best, bad → worse → worst.
blocks.heading: Degrees of Comparison

blocks.body:
Topic Level Description
Adjectives A1–A2 Basics of using adjectives
Adverbs A2–B1 Adverbs and their position
Degrees of Comparison A2–B1 Comparative and superlative forms
Order of Adjectives B1 The order of adjectives before a noun
Comparative Structures A2–B1 Comparison patterns such as as...as and than
Adjectives vs Adverbs A2–B1 The difference between adjectives and adverbs
blocks.heading: Topics in This Section

blocks.body:
• Full adjective order: Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Color → Origin → Material → Purpose.
  • Example: a beautiful small old round red Italian wooden dining table.
  • In practice, people rarely use more than 2-3 adjectives together.
blocks.heading: Order of Adjectives

blocks.body:
🧠

Start with basic adjectives because you use them all the time to describe things and people.

Remember: adverbs are often formed by adding -ly to an adjective.

For degrees of comparison, short words (1-2 syllables) usually take -er/-est, while long words use more/most.

Adjective order: opinion → size → age → color is one of the most common combinations.

Do not forget the irregular forms: good/better/best, bad/worse/worst, far/further/furthest.
blocks.heading: Study Tips
